tweets.text:
Etched has recruited legends from NVIDIA, Intel, Broadcom, Apple, and DeepMind:

- Brian Loiler: built NVIDIA's HGX and DGX systems from scratch over 22 years
 - Wayne Cao: led 0-to-1 production for the original iPhone
- David Munday: built the TPU software team at Google
- Saptadeep Pal: NVIDIA's H100 architecture team
- Mark Ross: ex-CTO of Cypress (acquired for $9.4B)

They recruit through a process they call project-based recruiting:

"We map out all of the hardest technical problems across all industries that anyone has ever had to solve. 

We look at who did the 0 to 1, who was actually in charge, who did the work.

We talked to as many people as possible, and then we just track it.

The amount of people who say yes after the first conversation is low, but the amount who say yes after the 20th is surprisingly high. 

The number one guy in the world versus the number ten versus the number one hundred is a huge difference in whether it's actually possible to solve the problem."

tweets.text:
I think a token launch is only as good as the mechanics behind it.

I’ve talked about @squidrouter before, and the $QUID Public Sale is one of the few events in crypto I've been excited about for the past few months.

ICYMI, Squid has been powering cross-chain infrastructure for top protocols like MetaMask or Ripple since 2022. They already routed $6B+ in volume, served 1M+ users, and support 100+ chains.

tweets.text:
Just some of the more interesting news I came across recently:

1. MS: Revised China humanoid shipments to reach 50,000 in 2026 from 14,000u and 28,000u earlier this year.

2. MS: Probe cards and test sockets likely to be priced hike (precious metal hikes + severe shortage of test pin capacity)

3. Capacitor price hikes: MLCC, aluminum electrolytic, tantalum, polymer aluminum, film, and super capacitors from Yageo.

4. $META Vistara architecture: Older DDR4 Memory with CXL as memory expansion

5. OpenAI achieved inference optimization breakthrough that halves costs and reduces GPU requirements. 

6. LTAs are being signed with MLCC after Samsung signed LTA with US big tech customer

7. Grid power bottlenecks triggered 5x increase ($25B) in Brookfield's financing for $BE fuel cells. 

8. Taiwan's chip packaging and testing supply chain includes major OSAT providers such as $ASX, Powertech, Winstek Semiconductor, Chipbond, ChipMOS, and ShunSin. 

Dedicated testing companies include KYEC, Ardentec, and Sigurd Microelectronics, while testing interface suppliers include WinWay, MPI, Keystone Microtech,  Chunghwa Precision, and Hermes Testing Solutions. 

Leadframe makers include Jih Lin Tech, SDI, and CWTC, while major IC substrate suppliers include Unimicron, Kinsus, Nan Ya PCB, and Zhen Ding Tech. (Digitimes) 

- Taiwan-based OSAT providers are raising prices
- Memory and IC packaging and testing capacity have both become bottlenecks in the semiconductor supply chain

tweets.text:
What's the best crypto card for you? (tldr I built an app to help you find out, scroll to the end if you just want to see it)

There are so many cards out there now that are promoting around crazy high cashback percentages. 4%, 6%, even some marketing 20% (lol)

While these numbers aren't *wrong*, they are often misleading and inaccurate for most people's actual usage

This is because of caps that are put in place for these cashback tiers. There's usually a monthly limit under which all spending is at a higher cashback rate, and after that, it drops to somewhere between 0.5 and 2%.

Companies also sometimes offer higher cashback for specific purposes, and market around that. For example, 10% cashback on travel, or 20% cashback on certain AI/gaming products.

Those caps are usually much smaller, and have little to do with most people's regular, everday spending.

The last thing these cashback percentages don't take into account are the monthly / yearly subscription fees. Cashback is great, but it usually comes with a cost (either paying a sub or staking/locking up tokens).

So to try and equalize all of this and find the best card for REAL cashback, I built an app that takes all of these things into account.

Introducing Crypto Card Finder, the best place to find REAL cashback based on your expected monthly spending (check us out at cryptocardfinder[dot]xyz)

It's super simple. Enter what you expect to spend per month, and we find you the best card (or cards) ranked purely on cashback rewards.

There are other things to consider of course (fx fees, lounge access, points programs / airdrops, insurance, other perks), but those are all intangible and tougher to equalize across everyone. At least looking at real cashback can give you a solid starting point from where to evaluate further.

The site is in beta mode and has a limited number of cards, but I will be looking to add more and more and make it the best and most comprehensive resource for evaluating crypto card cashback.

Please let me know if there's a card you want to see listed (ideally ones that are competitive with the existing ones, I will prioritize those).

Also please let me know if any of the information on the site is wrong or outdated, I had a look over everything but -- shock horror -- I did use AI to help with the building and research so some things may be a bit off. Always happy to update and correct info if pointed to a reliable source :)

Sharing some examples to look at here, dropping a link to the site in the post below

tweets.text:
The insider sales are probably the biggest thing that gives me pause. That said, I think it’s important to look at them in context rather than just the headline. The CEO still owns over 500,000 shares, and most of the other executives continue to hold meaningful stakes as well. None of them appear to be exiting the company or dumping a large percentage of their holdings.

It’s also worth remembering that the stock has appreciated significantly over the past few years. Executives diversify, exercise options, and sell shares for personal reasons all the time. It’s also my understanding that at least some of the CEO’s sales may have been related to his divorce settlement, although I haven’t independently verified that.

If I saw the CEO or multiple executives selling 30–50% of their holdings or completely exiting their positions, I’d be much more concerned. From what I’ve seen, that’s not what’s happening here, the CEO sold 4% of his holdings.

So yes, I view the insider selling as a yellow flag, and it’s something I continue to monitor closely. But by itself, I don’t think it’s enough to invalidate the investment thesis.

I think there���s a common misconception about what actually moves the stocks I write about.

I don’t believe retail investors are the primary reason these stocks move. My audience simply isn’t large enough for that in most cases.

What I do think happens is that my research brings overlooked opportunities to the attention of larger investors. I regularly receive private messages from hedge funds, family offices, and other professional investors who want to discuss a thesis or better understand my reasoning. They always do their own due diligence, as they should, but if they independently reach the same conclusion and decide to build meaningful positions, that’s what can move a stock.

My goal has never been to persuade people. It’s to present a simple, logical framework, remove as much emotion as possible, and look at a situation from every angle. If the thesis holds up under independent scrutiny, capital tends to follow.

I also don’t expect to be right 100% of the time. Nobody is. Investing is a game of probabilities, not certainties. That said, we’ve had a very strong run recently, and I believe that comes from constantly refining my process, learning from mistakes, and becoming a better investor every year.

The hedge fund framing seems to have become the fancy thing to say about MSTR this week but it has no basis, nothing changed this week, we've known for months now that they should be selling BTC to pay dividends in case the mNAV is too depressed to sell MSTR shares, that's very different from trying to time the market on selling BTC. Selling BTC to buy back assets that are correlated with it at a discount (MSTR + the prefs) is not the same thing at all as being a directional BTCUSD trader.

Also the PnL number is irrelevant. MSTR is a levered bet on BTC, so when BTC is down -50% in the middle of a bear market, MSTR is usually not in a comfortable position, framing this as a hedge fund who needs to trade out of a hole doesn't really make sense
